elements.sent-2411-001: The decay $H \to ZZ \to 4\ell$, with $\ell = e,\mu$, can be used to test quantum interference in analogy to the famous double-slit experiment.
elements.sent-2411-002: The observations corresponding to `covering a slit' can be extracted from data in the $ee\mu\mu$ channel, while the observations when both `slits' are open, which include the interference, are measured in the $4e/4\mu$ channels.
elements.sent-2411-003: Furthermore, this process offers a unique opportunity to investigate identical-particle effects at high energies, and provide the first evidence of identical-particle behaviour for muons.
elements.sent-2411-004: Sensitivity at the $4\sigma$ level could be achieved at the high-luminosity upgrade of the Large Hadron Collider.
elements.sent-2411-005: Quantum mechanics (QM) is essential for our understanding of Nature.
elements.sent-2411-006: When performing tests of QM at high energies we are probing its boundaries and thereby extending the range in which it is known to be a correct description of Nature.
elements.sent-2411-007: At present there is no compelling alternative to extend QM as formulated in the early 20$^\text{th}$ century; however, this fact does not diminish the importance of performing tests at high energies and under extreme conditions, where QM might start to break down.
elements.sent-2411-008: Quantum mechanics underlies quantum field theory, which successfully describes all particle interactions via the Standard Model.
elements.sent-2411-009: Therefore, all production and decay processes observed at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) are quantum in nature.
elements.sent-2411-010: Nonetheless, inherently quantum tests, such as those of entanglement already performed~\cite{ATLAS:2023fsd,CMS:2024pts,CMS:2024zkc , remain of significant interest to probe the foundations of quantum mechanics at high energy.}
elements.sent-2411-011: In this Letter we consider the Higgs boson decay $H \to ZZ$ in the four-lepton final state, highlighting its role as a novel test of identical-particle effects and quantum interference.
elements.sent-2411-012: Tests of identical-particle effects have already been performed with photons~\cite{Hong:1987zz and electrons~\cite{PhysRev.94.357 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: The $H \to ZZ \to 4 \ell$ process with $\ell = e,\mu$ not only provides a further test for electrons at higher energies, but, remarkably, the first test for muons, for which experimental evidence of their identical-particle behaviour is lacking.
elements.sent-2411-014: Moreover, $H \to ZZ \to 4 \ell$ demonstrates quantum interference, in a way analogous to the classic double-slit experiment.
elements.sent-2411-015: There are several magnitudes that differ between the decays into identical and distinguishable fermions, for example, the total decay width and the invariant mass distributions.
elements.sent-2411-016: In addition to those, some angular correlations exhibit striking differences.
elements.sent-2411-017: We will first set up our framework to study the angular correlations in the decay, to later discuss how to test interference and identical-particle effects.. {\em Framework.}
elements.sent-2411-018: For the sake of clarity we first discuss the $ee\mu\mu$ final state, where the particles are distinguishable and the two intermediate bosons can be uniquely identified by the flavour of their decay products.
elements.sent-2411-019: We will work in the helicity basis, a moving reference system with vectors $(\hat r, \hat n, \hat k)$ defined as follows~\cite{Bernreuther:2015yna :.}
elements.sent-2411-020: The other boson is labelled as $Z_2$, with decay products $\ell_2^+$, $\ell_2^-$.
elements.sent-2411-021: The angular orientation of the decay products can be specified by the polar and azimuthal angles $\Omega_1 = (\theta_1,\phi_1)$ and $\Omega_2 = (\theta_2,\phi_2)$ of the negative lepton momenta in the rest frame of the parent $Z$ boson, measured in the $(\hat r, \hat n, \hat k)$ reference system.
elements.sent-2411-022: The four-dimensional decay angular distribution reads~\cite{Aguilar-Saavedra:2022wam \begin{eqnarray}\frac{1}{\sigma}\frac{d\sigma}{d\Omega_1d\Omega_2} = \frac{1}{(4\pi)^2}\left[ 1 +a_{LM}^1 Y_L^M(\Omega_1) + a_{LM}^2 Y_L^M(\Omega_2) \right. \notag \left. + c_{L_1 M_1 L_2 M_2} Y_{L_1}^{M_1}(\Omega_1)Y_{L_2}^{M_2}(\Omega_2) \right] \,, \label{ec:dist4D}\end{eqnarray} with $Y_L^M$ the usual spherical harmonics, and implicit sum over repeated indices.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\frac{1}{\sigma}\frac{d\sigma}{d\Omega_1d\Omega_2} = \frac{1}{(4\pi)^2}\left[ 1 +a_{LM}^1 Y_L^M(\Omega_1) + a_{LM}^2 Y_L^M(\Omega_2) \right. \notag \left. + c_{L_1 M_1 L_2 M_2} Y_{L_1}^{M_1}(\Omega_1)Y_{L_2}^{M_2}(\Omega_2) \right] \,, \label{ec:dist4D}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-023: In the $ee\mu\mu$ final state with distinguishable particles the constants $a_{LM ^{1,2}$, $c_{L_1 M_1 L_2 M_2}$ are related to the coefficients in the spin density operator $\rho_{S_1 S_2}$ for the two $Z$ bosons~\cite{Aguilar-Saavedra:2022wam .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: Specifically, this operator can be written as an expansion in irreducible tensors $T^L_M$, \begin{eqnarray}\rho_{S_1 S_2} = \frac{1}{9}\left[ \mathbb{1}_3 \otimes \mathbb{1}_3 + A^1_{LM} T^L_M \otimes \mathbb{1}_3 + A^2_{LM} \mathbb{1}_3\otimes T^L_M \right. \notag \left. + C_{L_1 M_1 L_2 M_2}\ T^{L_1}_{M_1} \otimes T^{L_2}_{M_2} \right] \,, \label{rho}\end{eqnarray} with constants $A_{LM ^{1,2}$, $C_{L_1 M_1 L_2 M_2}$.}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\rho_{S_1 S_2} = \frac{1}{9}\left[ 1_3 \otimes 1_3 + A^1_{LM} T^L_M \otimes 1_3 + A^2_{LM} 1_3\otimes T^L_M \right. \notag \left. + C_{L_1 M_1 L_2 M_2}\ T^{L_1}_{M_1} \otimes T^{L_2}_{M_2} \right] \,, \label{rho}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-025: The precise definition of $T^L_M$ is not relevant here, and can be found e.g. in Ref.~\cite{Aguilar-Saavedra:2022wam .}
elements.sent-2411-026: For leptonic $Z$ decays one has $a_{LM ^{1,2 = B_L A_{LM ^{1,2 $, $c_{L_1 M_1 L_2 M_2 = B_{L_1 B_{L_2 C_{L_1 M_1 L_2 M_2}$, with $B_1 = - \sqrt{2\pi \eta_\ell$, $B_2 = \sqrt{2\pi/5}$, and \begin{equation}\eta_\ell = \frac{1-4 s_W^2}{1-4 s_W^2 + 8 s_W^4} \simeq 0.13 \,,\end{equation} $s_W$ being the sine of the weak angle~\cite{Aguilar-Saavedra:2017zkn .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The $\eta_\ell$ factor suppresses the coefficients of spherical harmonics with $L=1$ in the distribution (\ref{ec:dist4D ), especially $c_{1010}$ and $c_{111-1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-028: In $4e / 4\mu$ final states with identical particles there are two possible pairings between opposite-sign leptons.
elements.sent-2411-029: One can then {\em define the two $Z$ bosons by their momenta, $p_{Z_1 = p_{\ell_1^+ + p_{\ell_1^-}$, $p_{Z_2 = p_{\ell_2^+ + p_{\ell_2^-}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: And, as before, label as $Z_1$ the one with largest invariant mass.
elements.sent-2411-031: The reference system is then constructed as described for distinguishable particles, and likewise the four-dimensional decay distribution has the form (\ref{ec:dist4D ).}
elements.sent-2411-032: However, the numerical values of the coefficients $a_{LM ^{1,2}$ and $c_{L_1 M_1 L_2 M_2}$ differ from the $ee\mu\mu$ final state.}
elements.sent-2411-033: In particular, $c_{111-1}$ and $c_{1010}$ are up to 50 times larger, depending on the phase space region.
elements.sent-2411-034: We will precisely use the value of these coefficients to demonstrate the effects of interference and identical particles.
elements.sent-2411-035: In the $ee\mu\mu$ final state there is a `correct' pairing of opposite-sign leptons (by flavour), let us label it as `A'.
elements.sent-2411-037: We can regard each pairing as a `slit', and measure $c_{111-1}$ and $c_{1010}$ in each case.
elements.sent-2411-038: That would be the analogous to covering any of the two slits in the classical experiment.
elements.sent-2411-039: In addition, we can also perform the measurement counting each event twice, one for each pairing.
elements.sent-2411-040: The result obtained is the average of both pairings, which do not interfere.
elements.sent-2411-041: In the $4e/4\mu$ final states we can also count each event twice, one for each possible pairing.
elements.sent-2411-042: But the results obtained for $c_{111-1}$ and $c_{1010}$ are {\em not the average of pairings A and B, as in the $ee\mu\mu$ channel.}
elements.sent-2411-043: In other words, the observation for $4e/4\mu$ is not the sum of the observations for either of the two `slits' in $ee\mu\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-044: This is a clear indication of the interference.
elements.sent-2411-045: In terms of Feynman diagrams, each pairing --- or `slit' --- can be identified with a diagram, but note that our definitions and method to verify the interference do not rely on an expansion of the amplitude in terms of Feynman diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-046: This can be illustrated with a Monte Carlo calculation at the parton level.
elements.sent-2411-047: We generate $H \to ZZ \to 4\ell$ at the leading order with {\scshape MadGraph ~\cite{Alwall:2014hca .}}
elements.sent-2411-048: For $ee\mu\mu$ we obtain \begin{equation}Pairing A c_{111-1} = 0.098 c_{1010} = -0.059 \,, \notag Pairing B c_{111-1} = -1.210 c_{1010} = 14.244 \,, \notag Both pairings c_{111-1} = -0.556 c_{1010} = 7.093 \,.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}Pairing A c_{111-1} = 0.098 c_{1010} = -0.059 \,, \notag Pairing B c_{111-1} = -1.210 c_{1010} = 14.244 \,, \notag Both pairings c_{111-1} = -0.556 c_{1010} = 7.093 \,.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-049: As said, the result when counting each event twice is the average of pairings A and B.
elements.sent-2411-050: For $4e/4\mu$ we have \begin{equation}Both pairings c_{111-1} = -0.032 c_{1010} = 6.232 \,,\end{equation} which is not the average of pairings A and B, and exhibits the interference effect.
elements.sent-2411-051: The difference between the $4e/4\mu$ and $ee\mu\mu$ channels can also be studied as a function of $m_{Z_1}$, which is generally different for each pairing.
elements.sent-2411-052: The results are presented in Fig.~\ref{fig:c2-M , in 5 GeV bins.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Note that despite the fact that two pairings of a given event may in general lie in different bins, the differential distribution can be used as a probe of quantum interference.

elements.sent-2411-054: In order to have a genuine test, one has to apply the same criterion to pair opposite-sign leptons in the $ee\mu\mu$ and $4e/4\mu$ final states and compare some observable, for example the values of $c_{111-1}$, $c_{1010}$ obtained using both pairings.
elements.sent-2411-056: One simple but effective flavour-blind criterion that selects the correct pairing for $ee\mu\mu$ in the vast majority of the events is, among the two possibilities, to choose the one yielding the largest value of $m_{Z_1}$.
elements.sent-2411-057: We will denote this criterion as `mass pairing'.
elements.sent-2411-058: For low values of $m_{Z_1}$, i.e. when both bosons in $H \to ZZ \to ee\mu\mu$ are far from their mass shell, this criterion often fails to select the correct pairing; nevertheless, this phase space region amounts to a small fraction of the decay width.
elements.sent-2411-059: The kinematical distributions of $m_{Z_1}$ and $m_{Z_2}$ are presented in Fig.~\ref{fig:M .}
elements.sent-2411-060: The former already provides a good discriminant to test identical-particle effects.
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{center} \includegraphics[height=5.5cm,clip=]{Figs/M-Mpair.pdf} \caption{Kinematical distributions of $m_{Z_1}$ and $m_{Z_2}$, when applying mass pairing of opposite-sign leptons. } \label{fig:M} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-061: To improve the identification in the low-$m_{Z_1}$ region a boosted decision tree (BDT) can be trained using as input variables, for each of the two pairings (i) the two invariant masses of opposite-sign lepton pairs, $m_{Z_1}$, $m_{Z_2}$; (ii) the cosines of the angles between leptons in the same pair, $z_1$, $z_2$, taking the momenta in the Higgs boson rest frame.
elements.sent-2411-062: We implement this BDT using {\scshape XGboost ~\cite{Chen:2016:XST:2939672.2939785 with a maximum tree depth of 4, and a maximum of 100 trees, and otherwise default settings.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The BDT is trained on the $ee\mu\mu$ final state.
elements.sent-2411-064: For $m_{Z_1 \geq 80$ GeV the BDT identifies the correct pairing in more than 99\% of the events.}
elements.sent-2411-065: The percentage decreases to 85\% for $70 \leq m_{Z_1 \leq 75$ GeV, and drops to 58\% for $55 \leq m_{Z_1 \leq 60$ GeV.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: We find that a neural network obtains the same accuracy in the classification but with a much larger computing time.
elements.sent-2411-067: The distributions of $m_{Z_1}$ and $m_{Z_2}$ obtained with the BDT pairing are nearly identical to those in Fig.~\ref{fig:M .}

elements.sent-2411-075: We estimate the statistical sensitivity of these measurements at the high-luminosity upgrade of the LHC (HL-LHC), with a luminosity of 3 ab$^{-1}$.

elements.sent-2411-077: Hadronisation and parton showering are performed with {\scshape Pythia ~\cite{Sjostrand:2007gs and detector simulation with {\scshape Delphes ~\cite{deFavereau:2013fsa using the default card for the HL-LHC.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-079: Muons are selected with $|\eta| \leq 2.7$ and $p_T \geq 5$ GeV.
elements.sent-2411-080: We require two opposite-sign same-flavour leptons.
elements.sent-2411-081: The three higher-$p_T$ leptons must satisfy minimum thresholds of 20, 15 and 10 GeV, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-082: These event selection requirements are quite close to those in Ref.~\cite{ATLAS:2023mqy .}
elements.sent-2411-083: In addition, we select a window $120 \leq m_{4\ell \leq 130$ GeV for the four-lepton invariant mass.}
elements.sent-2411-084: In this region, the background has a size around 1/4 of the signal~\cite{ATLAS:2023mqy,CMS:2021ugl .}
elements.sent-2411-085: The expected number of events in the $ee\mu\mu$, $4e$ and $4\mu$ final states are 1144, 379 and 1106, respectively.

elements.sent-2411-089: The statistical uncertainty on $c_{111-1}$ and $c_{1010}$ is estimated by performing pseudo-experiments for each final state.
elements.sent-2411-090: In each pseudo-experiment, a subset of $N$ random events (the expected number of events for the final state considered) is drawn from the total sample, and for this subset the coefficients are determined from the distribution, without correction from detector-level to parton-level.
elements.sent-2411-091: A large number of $10^5$ pseudo-experiments is performed in order to obtain the probability density function (p.d.f.) of these quantities, and from it the statistical uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-092: For the calculation of $c_{111-1}$, $c_{1010}$ we also apply an upper cut $m_{Z_1 \leq M_Z$.}

elements.sent-2411-109: A final comment on the electroweak four-lepton background is in order.
elements.sent-2411-110: In the region of interest it is quite small, as mentioned; the coefficients are also quite similar in $ee\mu\mu$ and $4e/4\mu$ final states, as seen in Table~\ref{tab:creco .}
elements.sent-2411-111: And they are of similar magnitude as the ones for $H \to ZZ$.
elements.sent-2411-112: Therefore, a background subtraction, which is out of the scope of the present work, seems feasible, and will only result in a mild increase of the statistical uncertainty in $c_{111-1}$ and $c_{1010}$, and a corresponding decrease of the significance with respect to the results presented here.
elements.sent-2411-113: Next-to-leading order corrections for the $ee\mu\mu$ final state are known~\cite{Grossi:2024jae , and provide a small shift in $c_{111-1}$ and $c_{1010}$ which is well below the statistical uncertainty.. {\em Discussion.}}
elements.sent-2411-114: In this Letter we have proposed a novel probe of quantum interference in Higgs decays $H \to ZZ \to 4\ell$, based on angular correlations of the decay products.
elements.sent-2411-115: This test only relies on a data comparison between $ee\mu\mu$ and $4e/4\mu$ final states.
elements.sent-2411-116: This is in contrast to other proposals; for example, the ALICE Collaboration has measured an angular oscillating pattern in Pb-Pb collisions~\cite{ALICE:2024ife that can be interpreted as quantum interference but only after compared to Monte Carlo predictions.}
elements.sent-2411-117: Whereas, in Higgs decays one can even measure the analogous to `covering a slit' to subsequently establish the presence of quantum interference by using data alone.
elements.sent-2411-118: The measurements in Higgs decays can also be used to test identical-particle effects.
elements.sent-2411-119: To our knowledge, these would be the first tests to establish the identical-particle behaviour for muons.
elements.sent-2411-120: We have explored several observables that differ between $ee\mu\mu$ and $4e/4\mu$ final states, to conclude that the angular correlations used to probe quantum interference are also the most sensitive to establish the identical-particle behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-121: We have not considered the total decay width, whose difference between $ee\mu\mu$ and $4e/4\mu$ final states does not have a clear interpretation as due to identical-particle behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-122: In our work we have focused, for simplicity, on the angular correlation coefficients $c_{111-1}$ and $c_{1010}$.
elements.sent-2411-123: Other terms in the distribution (\ref{ec:dist4D ) exhibit differences between $ee\mu\mu$ and $4e/4\mu$ final states, and they could also be incorporated in an experimental analysis to improve the statistical significance of the discrimination.}
elements.sent-2411-124: For example, $a_{20 ^{1,2}$ show differences at the $1\sigma-2\sigma$ level, while the differences in $c_{222-2}$, $c_{212-1}$ and $c_{2020}$ are below $1\sigma$.}
elements.sent-2411-125: Electroweak $ZZ$ production with both $Z$ bosons on their mass shell has a larger cross section than $H \to ZZ$, but is not useful to probe interference and identical-particle effects.
elements.sent-2411-126: The correlation coefficients are very similar in $ee\mu\mu$ and $4e/4\mu$ final states, with differences at the few percent level.
elements.sent-2411-127: This is due to the small contribution of the interference between diagrams related by identical-particle exchange in the latter.
elements.sent-2411-128: Below the $ZZ$ threshold, four-lepton production can be mediated by virtual $Z$ and $\gamma$ exchange; however, the differences between $ee\mu\mu$ and $4e/4\mu$ final states are small --- see for example Table~\ref{tab:creco for the kinematical region near the Higgs peak.}
elements.sent-2411-129: Then, $H \to ZZ \to 4\ell$, despite its small cross section, seems to be the most suited process to probe quantum interference and identical-particle effects, with an expected sensitivity at the $4\sigma$ level at the HL-LHC.
elements.sent-2411-130: I thank Y.. Afik and A.

elements.sent-2411-133: In this way, this process offers a unique opportunity to investigate identical-particle effects at high energies, and provide the first evidence of identical-particle behaviour for muons.
elements.sent-2411-134: The reference system is then constructed as described for distinguishable particles, and likewise the four-dimensional decay distribution has the form (\ref{ec:dist4D ).\footnote{In principle, higher-rank spherical harmonics may enter the angular distribution for $4e/4\mu$ final states.}}
elements.sent-2411-135: We have checked that the coefficients for $L=3$ are compatible with zero within Monte Carlo uncertainty (for several million events).
elements.sent-2411-136: This can be understood since a fermion pair in s-wave has total angular momentum $J=0,1$, and behaves like a scalar or spin-1 particle.
elements.sent-2411-137: But one can also consider a `wrong' pairing, labelled as `B', in which we define $p_{X_1 = p_{e^+ + p_{\mu^-}$, $p_{X_2 = p_{\mu^+ + p_{e^-}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-138: Neither $X_1$ nor $X_2$ correspond to any intermediate $Z$ boson in this final state, but $p_{X_i}$ and $p_{X_2}$ are time-like momenta, so we can boost the $\mu^-$ and $e^-$ momenta to the $X_1$ and $X_2$ rest frames, respectively, define the angles $\Omega_{1,2}$ and from the distribution (1) calculate coefficients $c_{111-1}$ and $c_{1010}$ --- which admittedly do not have any interpretation in terms of spin.
elements.sent-2411-139: In this case, the result obtained is the average of the results obtained for each pairing, i.e. an incoherent sum.
elements.sent-2411-140: In $4e/4\mu$ final states there is no `correct' pairing but we can still count each event twice, one for each pairing.
elements.sent-2411-141: And the results obtained for $c_{111-1}$ and $c_{1010}$ are {\em not the average of pairings A and B as obtained for the $ee\mu\mu$ channel.}
elements.sent-2411-142: In other words, the observation for $4e/4\mu$ is not the incoherent sum of the observations for the two choices in $ee\mu\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-143: This discussion can be illustrated with a Monte Carlo calculation at the parton level in full phase space.
elements.sent-2411-144: The analogy with the double-slit experiment follows since in $4e/4\mu$ final states each lepton pairing is a possibility (e.g. to reconstruct the virtual $Z$ bosons), which can be regarded as a `slit'.
elements.sent-2411-145: Physical results are the coherent sum of both, as it is evident from the amplitude expansion in Feynman diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-146: In order to make the interference effect manifest we have considered the coefficients $c_{111-1}$, $c_{1010}$ obtained when summing over both pairings, and compared with the same quantity for the $ee\mu\mu$ final state.
elements.sent-2411-147: Following the analogy, the result of `covering a slit', that is, the result obtained with only one pairing, can be experimentally measured in $ee\mu\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-148: And it is found that in $4e/4\mu$ the result is not the average of both, again in analogy with the double-slit experiment.

elements.sent-2411-149: We consider $gg \to H \to ZZ \to 4 \ell$ at 14 TeV, normalising the Higgs cross section to 54.67 pb~\cite{Cepeda:2019klc and using branching ratios to $ee\mu\mu$ and $4e/4\mu$ of $5.9 \times 10^{-5}$ and $6.5 \times 10^{-5}$, respectively~\cite{LHCHiggsCrossSectionWorkingGroup:2016ypw .}}
elements.sent-2411-150: Next-to-leading order corrections for the $ee\mu\mu$ final state are known~\cite{Grossi:2024jae , and provide a small shift in $c_{111-1}$ and $c_{1010}$ which is below the statistical uncertainty.. {\em Discussion.}}
